title: 8.3 Rational functions, partial fractions, and Vieta formulas
description: Decompose rational functions into simpler fractions, relate roots to coefficients, and use Vieta formulas in trigonometric polynomial problems.

body.raw:
## Why the final part of the polynomial chapter matters

Polynomial division and gcds are not isolated techniques. They support two
large tools that appear throughout later mathematics:

- partial fraction decomposition, which rewrites rational functions into
  simpler pieces;
- root-coefficient relations, especially Vieta's formulas, which let us compute
  symmetric expressions in roots without solving for the roots one by one.

Both tools depend on earlier results. Partial fractions need polynomial
division, factorization, and relative primality. Vieta's formulas need the
factorization of a polynomial over `C`.

## Rational functions and the polynomial part

<Definition title="Rational function">
A rational function is a quotient

$$
\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}
$$

where `p(x),q(x)\in R[x]` and `q(x)\ne0`.
</Definition>

The first step in any partial fraction problem is to separate the polynomial
part. If `\deg p\ge \deg q`, the division algorithm gives

$$
p(x)=q(x)b(x)+r(x),\qquad \deg r\lt\deg q.
$$

Therefore

$$
\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}=b(x)+\frac{r(x)}{q(x)}.
$$

Only the proper rational function `r(x)/q(x)` still needs decomposition.

## Splitting relatively prime denominator factors

Suppose

$$
q(x)=q_1(x)q_2(x)
$$

and `\gcd(q_1,q_2)=1`. For a proper rational function `r(x)/q(x)`, Bezout's
identity gives polynomials `a(x),b(x)` with

$$
a(x)q_1(x)+b(x)q_2(x)=1.
$$

Multiplying by `r(x)` and dividing by `q_1q_2` gives

$$
\frac{r(x)}{q_1(x)q_2(x)}
=\frac{r(x)b(x)}{q_1(x)}
+\frac{r(x)a(x)}{q_2(x)}.
$$

Then divide the numerators by `q_1` and `q_2` to reduce their degrees. The
result is a unique decomposition

$$
\frac{r(x)}{q(x)}
=\frac{r_1(x)}{q_1(x)}
+\frac{r_2(x)}{q_2(x)},
\qquad
\deg r_i\lt\deg q_i.
$$

This is the structural reason partial fractions work.

<TheoremCard title="Partial fraction shape over R">
If the denominator factors as

$$
K\prod_i(x-a_i)^{m_i}\prod_j(x^2+b_jx+c_j)^{n_j},
$$

where the quadratic factors are irreducible over `R`, then every rational
function can be written uniquely as a polynomial plus terms of the forms

$$
\frac{k}{(x-a)^m},
\qquad
\frac{rx+s}{(x^2+bx+c)^n}.
$$
</TheoremCard>

Repeated linear factors require one constant numerator for each power:

$$
\frac{A(x)}{(x-a)^m}
=\frac{k_1}{x-a}+\frac{k_2}{(x-a)^2}+\cdots+\frac{k_m}{(x-a)^m}.
$$

Repeated irreducible quadratic factors require linear numerators:

$$
\frac{B(x)}{(x^2+bx+c)^n}
=\sum_{j=1}^n\frac{r_jx+s_j}{(x^2+bx+c)^j}.
$$

<WorkedExample title="Set up a partial fraction decomposition">
Resolve

$$
\frac{x^4+2x+4}{2x^3-4x^2+3x-6}
$$

into partial fractions.

First divide:

$$
\frac{x^4+2x+4}{2x^3-4x^2+3x-6}
=\frac12x+1+
\frac{\frac52x^2+2x+10}{(2x^2+3)(x-2)}.
$$

Then write

$$
\frac{\frac52x^2+2x+10}{(2x^2+3)(x-2)}
=\frac{Ax+B}{2x^2+3}+\frac{C}{x-2}.
$$

Comparing coefficients gives

$$
A=-\frac{41}{22},\qquad B=-\frac{19}{11},\qquad C=\frac{24}{11}.
$$

Thus

$$
\frac{x^4+2x+4}{2x^3-4x^2+3x-6}
=\frac12x+1-\frac{41x+38}{22(2x^2+3)}
+\frac{24}{11(x-2)}.
$$
</WorkedExample>

## Telescoping from partial fractions

Partial fractions often convert a complicated finite sum into a telescoping
one. The slides use

$$
\frac{x}{(2x-1)(2x+1)(2x+3)}
=\frac{1}{16(2x-1)}+\frac{1}{8(2x+1)}
-\frac{3}{16(2x+3)}.
$$

Substituting `x=k` and summing from `k=1` to `n` makes most middle terms cancel.
The remaining boundary terms are

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n}
\frac{k}{(2k-1)(2k+1)(2k+3)}
=\frac1{16}\left(2-\frac1{2n+1}-\frac3{2n+3}\right).
$$

The main lesson is not the particular numbers; it is the method. Decompose
first, then check whether shifted denominator patterns cancel in a finite sum.

## Vieta's formulas

Let

$$
p(x)=a_nx^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1x+a_0\in C[x],
\qquad a_n\ne0.
$$

By the fundamental theorem of algebra,

$$
p(x)=a_n(x-\alpha_1)(x-\alpha_2)\cdots(x-\alpha_n),
$$

where the roots are counted with multiplicity.

<TheoremCard title="Vieta's formulas">
For `j=1,2,\ldots,n`,

$$
\sum_{1\le i_1\lt\cdots<i_j\le n}
\alpha_{i_1}\alpha_{i_2}\cdots\alpha_{i_j}
=(-1)^j\frac{a_{n-j}}{a_n}.
$$
</TheoremCard>

For a cubic

$$
a_3x^3+a_2x^2+a_1x+a_0,
$$

with roots `\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3`, this says

$$
\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3=-\frac{a_2}{a_3},
$$

$$
\alpha_1\alpha_2+\alpha_1\alpha_3+\alpha_2\alpha_3=\frac{a_1}{a_3},
$$

$$
\alpha_1\alpha_2\alpha_3=-\frac{a_0}{a_3}.
$$

<WorkedExample title="Power sums for cubic roots">
Let the roots of

$$
a_0+a_1x+a_2x^2+a_3x^3=0
$$

be `\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3`, and write
`S_r=\alpha_1^r+\alpha_2^r+\alpha_3^r`.

Then

$$
S_2=(\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3)^2
-2(\alpha_1\alpha_2+\alpha_1\alpha_3+\alpha_2\alpha_3)
=\frac{a_2^2-2a_1a_3}{a_3^2}.
$$

Similarly,

$$
S_3=
\frac{-a_2^3+3a_1a_2a_3-3a_0a_3^2}{a_3^3}.
$$

The useful habit is to rewrite nonsymmetric-looking expressions in terms of
symmetric sums that Vieta controls.
</WorkedExample>

## Optional interpolation viewpoint

The slide chapter ends with Lagrange interpolation. If

$$
(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_{n+1},y_{n+1})
$$

have distinct `x_i`, then there is a unique polynomial of degree at most `n`
passing through those `n+1` points.

For each `i`, define

$$
L_i(x)=
\prod_{j\ne i}\frac{x-x_j}{x_i-x_j}.
$$

Then `L_i(x_j)=\delta_{ij}`, so

$$
f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}y_iL_i(x)
$$

has the required values. Uniqueness follows from the root bound: the difference
of two such polynomials has degree at most `n` and `n+1` roots, so it must be
zero.

## Proof sketch or proof idea

Partial fraction decomposition is not a memorized list of templates. The
templates are the final form of three earlier facts working together. First,
the division algorithm removes the polynomial part. If the fraction is
improper, there is a polynomial part `b(x)` and a smaller proper fraction
`r(x)/q(x)`.

Second, relatively prime denominator factors can be separated by Bezout's
identity. If `q=q_1q_2` and `\gcd(q_1,q_2)=1`, then `a q_1+b q_2=1`.
Multiplying by the numerator and dividing by `q` splits the fraction into a
term over `q_1` and a term over `q_2`. This explains why distinct relatively
prime factors receive separate partial-fraction terms.

Third, repeated powers are peeled off by division. A numerator above
`(x-a)^m` can be divided by `x-a`, leaving a constant remainder over the top
power and a smaller-power fraction. Repeating this produces one term for each
power. The same idea works for irreducible quadratics, except the remainder has
degree less than `2`, so it has the form `rx+s`.

Vieta's formulas have a different but equally structural proof. Once the
fundamental theorem of algebra writes `p(x)=a_n\prod_{i=1}^{n}(x-\alpha_i)`,
the coefficient of each power of `x` is an elementary symmetric sum in the
roots. Comparing coefficients gives the formulas. This is why Vieta controls
symmetric expressions in roots, but not arbitrary expressions unless they can
be rewritten symmetrically.

<WorkedExample title="Read a partial-fraction form before solving coefficients">
Write the correct partial-fraction form for

$$
\frac{2x^2+1}{x^2(x^2+1)^2}
$$

over `R`.

The factor `x^2` is a repeated linear factor, so it contributes

$$
\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x^2}.
$$

The factor `x^2+1` is an irreducible quadratic over `R`, and it is repeated to
power `2`, so it contributes

$$
\frac{Cx+D}{x^2+1}+\frac{Ex+F}{(x^2+1)^2}.
$$

Thus the full form is

$$
\frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x^2}
+\frac{Cx+D}{x^2+1}
+\frac{Ex+F}{(x^2+1)^2}.
$$

At this stage we are not solving for `A,B,C,D,E,F`. We are first making sure
the shape has every required denominator power and the correct numerator
degree.
</WorkedExample>

<WorkedExample title="Use Vieta in the tangent capstone">
Exercise 8.3 reduces the three numbers

$$
\tan^2(\pi/9),\quad \tan^2(2\pi/9),\quad \tan^2(4\pi/9)
$$

to the roots of `x^3-33x^2+27x-3=0`. Let these roots be
`\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3`. Vieta's formulas give

$$
\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3=33,\qquad
\alpha_1\alpha_2+\alpha_1\alpha_3+\alpha_2\alpha_3=27,
$$

and

$$
\alpha_1\alpha_2\alpha_3=3.
$$

Since the three tangent values are positive, their product is the positive
square root of `3`:

$$
\tan(\pi/9)\tan(2\pi/9)\tan(4\pi/9)=\sqrt3.
$$

For the sixth-power sum, rewrite
`\alpha_1^3+\alpha_2^3+\alpha_3^3` as

$$
(\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3)^3
-3(\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3)(\alpha_1\alpha_2+\alpha_1\alpha_3+\alpha_2\alpha_3)
+3\alpha_1\alpha_2\alpha_3.
$$

Substitution gives `33^3-3(33)(27)+3(3)=33273`.
</WorkedExample>

## Common mistakes

<CommonMistake title="Skipping denominator powers">
For a repeated factor such as `(x-2)^4`, one term over `(x-2)^4` is not enough.
The form must include every power from `1` through `4`.
</CommonMistake>

<CommonMistake title="Using constant numerators over irreducible quadratics">
Over `R`, a term over `x^2+1` needs a numerator of degree less than `2`, so the
general numerator is `Ax+B`, not just a constant.
</CommonMistake>

<CommonMistake title="Applying Vieta to non-symmetric expressions directly">
Vieta gives sums of products of roots, not arbitrary individual roots. Before
using it on a power sum or trigonometric product, rewrite the expression in
terms of symmetric sums.
</CommonMistake>

## Summary

This note uses the algebraic machinery from the first two polynomial notes.
Division separates polynomial and proper-rational parts. Bezout identities and
relative primality explain why denominator factors split. Repeated powers
determine the number of partial-fraction terms. Vieta's formulas compare the
root-factor form of a complex polynomial with its coefficient form, allowing
root sums and products to be computed without solving the polynomial.

## Study guide for the exercises

For partial fractions, always decide the form before solving for coefficients.
This prevents two common mistakes: omitting a repeated power and using a
constant numerator where a linear numerator is needed. A repeated linear factor
`(x-a)^m` contributes one term for each denominator power. A repeated
irreducible quadratic contributes one linear numerator for each denominator
power. Only after the form is correct should you clear denominators and compare
coefficients.

When coefficient comparison becomes messy, organize it by powers of `x`.
After clearing denominators, expand the right-hand side and collect the
coefficient of each power. A cubic identity gives four scalar equations, one
for each of `x^3`, `x^2`, `x`, and the constant term. The system may look
long, but it is ordinary linear algebra in the unknown coefficients of the
partial fractions.

For telescoping sums, do not expect cancellation until after the summand has
been decomposed. The original rational expression may show no obvious
cancellation. Once it is written as a combination of shifted simple fractions,
write out the first few and last few terms. The middle terms should reveal
which pieces cancel and which boundary terms remain.

For Vieta problems, identify the polynomial and name its roots before doing
any computation. Then list the elementary symmetric sums that Vieta gives:
the sum of roots, the sum of pairwise products, and the full product in the
cubic case. If the target expression is a power sum, rewrite it using these
symmetric sums. If the target expression involves square roots of the roots,
as in the tangent exercise, decide the sign separately from the product of the
squared values.

The optional interpolation section is included because it uses the same
zero-count theorem from 8.1. It is not a new algebraic technique to memorize;
it is an application of the idea that a low-degree polynomial is determined by
enough point values. This same uniqueness logic appears in numerical methods,
curve fitting, and approximation theory.

One final habit helps throughout the chapter: after every decomposition or
Vieta computation, state what has actually been proved. A partial fraction
form proves equality of rational functions only where the original denominator
is nonzero. A Vieta computation proves a relation among roots counted with
multiplicity. Keeping these conditions visible prevents algebraic formulas from
being used outside their valid domain.

## Quick checks

<QuickCheck prompt="Why do we first divide `p(x)` by `q(x)` before doing partial fractions?">
Check the degree requirement on the remaining fraction.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
Partial fraction shapes are for proper rational functions. Division separates
the polynomial part and leaves a remainder `r(x)` with `\deg r\lt\deg q`.
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="What numerator shape belongs above an irreducible quadratic factor such as `x^2+1`?">
Use the degree rule for the numerator.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
The numerator should be linear, so it has the form `Ax+B`.
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="For `x^3-33x^2+27x-3`, what is the product of the three roots?">
Use the cubic Vieta formula.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
For a monic cubic, the product is `-a_0`. Here `a_0=-3`, so the product is
`3`.
</RevealSolution>

## Exercises

1. Write the correct partial fraction form for
   `5/(x^2+x-6)`.
2. Write the correct partial fraction form for
   `(2x^2+1)/(x^2(x^2+1)^2)`.
3. Resolve
   `x/((2x-1)(2x+1)(2x+3))` into partial fractions.
4. Use the result of exercise 3 to evaluate
   `\sum_{k=1}^n k/((2k-1)(2k+1)(2k+3))`.
5. Let `\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3` be the roots of
   `x^3-33x^2+27x-3`. Compute
   `\alpha_1+\alpha_2+\alpha_3`,
   `\alpha_1\alpha_2+\alpha_1\alpha_3+\alpha_2\alpha_3`, and
   `\alpha_1\alpha_2\alpha_3`.
6. Following Exercise 8.3 from the slides, suppose
   `\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3` are
   `\tan^2(\pi/9)`, `\tan^2(2\pi/9)`, and `\tan^2(4\pi/9)`, and they are the
   roots of `x^3-33x^2+27x-3`. Deduce
   `\tan(\pi/9)\tan(2\pi/9)\tan(4\pi/9)`.

<RevealSolution title="Guided solutions">

1. Since `x^2+x-6=(x+3)(x-2)`, the form is
   `A/(x+3)+B/(x-2)`.
2. The form is
   `A/x+B/x^2+(Cx+D)/(x^2+1)+(Ex+F)/(x^2+1)^2`.
3. The decomposition is
   `1/[16(2x-1)]+1/[8(2x+1)]-3/[16(2x+3)]`.
4. Substitute `x=k`, sum, and align shifted odd denominators. The result is
   `1/16(2-1/(2n+1)-3/(2n+3))`.
5. Vieta gives `33`, `27`, and `3`, respectively.
6. The product of the three squared tangent values is `3`. Since each tangent
   is positive for the angles shown, the product of the tangents is `\sqrt3`.
</RevealSolution>

`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`Partial fraction decomposition is not a memorized list of templates. The
templates are the final form of three earlier facts working together. First,
the division algorithm removes the polynomial part. If the fraction is
improper, there is a polynomial part `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"b(x)"}),` and a smaller proper fraction
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"r(x)/q(x)"}),"."]}),`
`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`Second, relatively prime denominator factors can be separated by Bezout's
identity. If `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"q=q_1q_2"})," and ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"\\gcd(q_1,q_2)=1"}),", then ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"a q_1+b q_2=1"}),`.
Multiplying by the numerator and dividing by `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"q"}),` splits the fraction into a
term over `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"q_1"})," and a term over ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"q_2"}),`. This explains why distinct relatively
prime factors receive separate partial-fraction terms.`]}),`
`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`Third, repeated powers are peeled off by division. A numerator above
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"(x-a)^m"})," can be divided by ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x-a"}),`, leaving a constant remainder over the top
power and a smaller-power fraction. Repeating this produces one term for each
power. The same idea works for irreducible quadratics, except the remainder has
degree less than `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"2"}),", so it has the form ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"rx+s"}),"."]}),`
`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`Vieta's formulas have a different but equally structural proof. Once the
fundamental theorem of algebra writes `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"p(x)=a_n\\prod_{i=1}^{n}(x-\\alpha_i)"}),`,
the coefficient of each power of `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x"}),` is an elementary symmetric sum in the
roots. Comparing coefficients gives the formulas. This is why Vieta controls
symmetric expressions in roots, but not arbitrary expressions unless they can
be rewritten symmetrically.`]}),`

`,(0,s.jsx)(l,{title:"Skipping denominator powers",children:(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["For a repeated factor such as ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"(x-2)^4"}),", one term over ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"(x-2)^4"}),` is not enough.

general numerator is `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"Ax+B"}),", not just a constant."]})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(l,{title:"Applying Vieta to non-symmetric expressions directly",children:(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`Vieta gives sums of products of roots, not arbitrary individual roots. Before
using it on a power sum or trigonometric product, rewrite the expression in
terms of symmetric sums.`})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.h2,{children:"Summary"}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`This note uses the algebraic machinery from the first two polynomial notes.
Division separates polynomial and proper-rational parts. Bezout identities and
relative primality explain why denominator factors split. Repeated powers
determine the number of partial-fraction terms. Vieta's formulas compare the
root-factor form of a complex polynomial with its coefficient form, allowing
root sums and products to be computed without solving the polynomial.`}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.h2,{children:"Study guide for the exercises"}),`
`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`For partial fractions, always decide the form before solving for coefficients.
This prevents two common mistakes: omitting a repeated power and using a
constant numerator where a linear numerator is needed. A repeated linear factor
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"(x-a)^m"}),` contributes one term for each denominator power. A repeated
irreducible quadratic contributes one linear numerator for each denominator
power. Only after the form is correct should you clear denominators and compare
coefficients.`]}),`
`,(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["When coefficient comparison becomes messy, organize it by powers of ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x"}),`.
After clearing denominators, expand the right-hand side and collect the
coefficient of each power. A cubic identity gives four scalar equations, one
for each of `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x^3"}),", ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x^2"}),", ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"x"}),`, and the constant term. The system may look
long, but it is ordinary linear algebra in the unknown coefficients of the
partial fractions.`]}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`For telescoping sums, do not expect cancellation until after the summand has
been decomposed. The original rational expression may show no obvious
cancellation. Once it is written as a combination of shifted simple fractions,
write out the first few and last few terms. The middle terms should reveal
which pieces cancel and which boundary terms remain.`}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`For Vieta problems, identify the polynomial and name its roots before doing
any computation. Then list the elementary symmetric sums that Vieta gives:
the sum of roots, the sum of pairwise products, and the full product in the
cubic case. If the target expression is a power sum, rewrite it using these
symmetric sums. If the target expression involves square roots of the roots,
as in the tangent exercise, decide the sign separately from the product of the
squared values.`}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`The optional interpolation section is included because it uses the same
zero-count theorem from 8.1. It is not a new algebraic technique to memorize;
it is an application of the idea that a low-degree polynomial is determined by
enough point values. This same uniqueness logic appears in numerical methods,
curve fitting, and approximation theory.`}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:`One final habit helps throughout the chapter: after every decomposition or
Vieta computation, state what has actually been proved. A partial fraction
form proves equality of rational functions only where the original denominator
is nonzero. A Vieta computation proves a relation among roots counted with
multiplicity. Keeping these conditions visible prevents algebraic formulas from
being used outside their valid domain.`}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.h2,{children:"Quick checks"}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(n,{prompt:"Why do we first divide `p(x)` by `q(x)` before doing partial fractions?",children:(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Check the degree requirement on the remaining fraction."})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(c,{title:"Answer",children:(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:[`Partial fraction shapes are for proper rational functions. Division separates
the polynomial part and leaves a remainder `,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"r(x)"})," with ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"\\deg r\\lt\\deg q"}),"."]})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(n,{prompt:"What numerator shape belongs above an irreducible quadratic factor such as `x^2+1`?",children:(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Use the degree rule for the numerator."})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(c,{title:"Answer",children:(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["The numerator should be linear, so it has the form ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"Ax+B"}),"."]})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(n,{prompt:"For `x^3-33x^2+27x-3`, what is the product of the three roots?",children:(0,s.jsx)(e.p,{children:"Use the cubic Vieta formula."})}),`
`,(0,s.jsx)(c,{title:"Answer",children:(0,s.jsxs)(e.p,{children:["For a monic cubic, the product is ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"-a_0"}),". Here ",(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"a_0=-3"}),`, so the product is
`,(0,s.jsx)(e.code,{children:"3"}),"."]})}),`
